sections.subtitle: Team Form
sections.text: Sunderland have shown strong form in their last 5 matches, securing 3 wins, 1 draw, and 1 loss, with a recent 2-1 victory over Chelsea highlighting their attacking potential. Everton, on the other hand, have struggled, managing just 1 win, 1 draw, and 3 losses, including a 0-3 defeat to Tottenham. This disparity in recent results gives Sunderland a clear edge heading into the match.

sections.subtitle: Head-to-Head Record
sections.text: Historically, Everton have dominated Sunderland in their last encounters, winning both of the most recent head-to-head matches (3-0 and 2-0) without conceding a goal. Despite this, the matches were played in 2017, and current form suggests Sunderland could challenge this trend at the Stadium of Light.

sections.subtitle: Season Statistics
sections.text: Sunderland, sitting 7th in the league with 17 points, boast a solid defense, conceding just 7 goals (2nd best in the league), while their ground duels won percentage (54.1%) is the highest in the division. Everton, languishing in 15th with 11 points, have struggled defensively, conceding in all of their last 6 matches. Sunderland’s defensive stability and home advantage could be key factors.

sections.subtitle: Key Insights
sections.text: Sunderland’s ability to score late (5 goals between 76-90 minutes this season) could exploit Everton’s vulnerability in the closing stages, as seen in their recent losses. Everton’s lack of clean sheets and tendency to concede first (5 out of 6 matches) further tilts the balance towards the hosts. However, Everton’s historical edge in head-to-heads might give them some psychological boost.

sections.subtitle: Match Prediction
sections.text: Given Sunderland’s superior form, defensive solidity, and home advantage, coupled with Everton’s recent struggles, the Sofascore prediction of a 55% chance for a home win seems plausible. A narrow victory for Sunderland appears likely, though Everton could capitalize on any defensive lapses. Expect a competitive match with limited goals due to Sunderland’s low-scoring trend (less than 2.5 goals in 5 of 6 recent games).

halftimeSections.subtitle: Half-time overview
halftimeSections.text: At half-time, Everton leads Sunderland 1-0 at the Stadium of Light. The solitary goal came early from Iliman Ndiaye, giving the visitors a deserved edge. Sunderland, despite dominating possession, have struggled to create clear-cut chances, failing to register a single shot on target. Everton, on the other hand, capitalized on their opportunity and showed more threat in the final third, even hitting the woodwork once.

halftimeSections.subtitle: Sunderland's first-half performance
halftimeSections.text: Sunderland controlled the ball for the majority of the first half, enjoying a significant share of possession. Their frequent entries into the final third and penalty area highlight their intent to attack. However, they’ve been unable to convert this territorial advantage into goal-scoring opportunities. Defensively, they’ve been solid, winning most duels and aerial battles, but their lack of cutting edge in attack remains a concern. Discipline has also been an issue with a couple of yellow cards picked up.

halftimeSections.subtitle: Everton's first-half performance
halftimeSections.text: Everton made the most of their limited possession, striking early to take the lead. Their attacking efficiency stood out, creating a big chance and converting one into a goal. Despite being second-best in terms of ball control and duels, they’ve been more clinical in the final third. Defensively, they’ve held firm, with several key clearances helping to protect their lead. They’ve also shown some attacking intent by hitting the woodwork, though they did miss a big chance to extend their advantage.

halftimeSections.subtitle: Attack momentum analysis
halftimeSections.text: The first half saw fluctuating attacking momentum between the two sides. Early on, Everton had several sustained spells of pressure, particularly in the opening minutes, which culminated in their goal around the quarter-hour mark. Sunderland responded with brief moments of attacking intent but couldn’t maintain consistent pressure. As the half progressed, Everton enjoyed longer periods of dominance in attack, especially in the middle stages. Towards the end of the half, Sunderland pushed hard with a significant shift in momentum, mounting a series of attacks, but they couldn’t break through Everton’s defense.

halftimeSections.subtitle: Key players to watch
halftimeSections.text: For Sunderland, players like Daniel Ballard have been strong defensively, while Nordi Mukiele and Enzo Le Fée have shown promise in creating opportunities. Up front, Wilson Isidor will need to step up to convert chances if Sunderland are to equalize. For Everton, Iliman Ndiaye has already made his mark with the opening goal and remains a constant threat. Jack Grealish’s creativity in the final third has been vital, and Thierno Barry, despite missing a big chance, could be dangerous if given space in the second half.

halftimeSections.subtitle: Second-half expectations
halftimeSections.text: Sunderland are likely to come out with renewed vigor, pushing for an equalizer with their home crowd behind them. Their dominance in possession and duels suggests they can create chances if they sharpen their final ball. Everton, meanwhile, will aim to defend their lead and exploit counter-attacking opportunities, as they’ve shown clinical finishing so far. The second half could see a more open game if Sunderland commit numbers forward, potentially leaving gaps for Everton to exploit.
